content.assessment: From a demographic lens, this admissions-redesign proposal operates entirely within a shrinking-cohort environment (F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02) but does not itself engage cohort size or geography as design variables. Its coaching-resistance mechanism targets sorting quality, not sorting quantity, so it leaves untouched the structural viability question of whether enough students exist locally to sustain 4/6/8-year pipelines. Given that selective-track uptake is already concentrated in urban/county-seat schools while 40-60% of rural schools see zero competition entrants (F-live-demography-03), a nationally uniform exam redesign will interact with a geographically skewed applicant pool: it may improve fairness among those who already apply from well-served areas without altering the rural non-participation baseline. The proposal's own risk register acknowledges that admissions-mechanism fixes may not reach non-applicant pools, which aligns with the demographic finding that rural elementary schools often do not facilitate entry-competition participation at all.
opportunities: Could be paired with geographic weighting to compensate for the documented urban clustering of applicants, addressing part of the spatial distribution criterion
strengths: Uses an existing centrally-graded exam lever, requiring no new institutional capacity in a period of demographic strain (consistent with F-live-demography-09 workforce constraints)
Does not add new selective-track infrastructure, avoiding exacerbation of viability problems in shrinking rural cohorts (F-live-demography-04)
threats: Without a structural or geographic complement, continued cohort decline (F-live-demography-01, F-live-demography-02) will erode the pool of applicants faster in rural areas than urban ones, potentially making the redesigned exam increasingly irrelevant outside urban centers over a 20-year horizon
weaknesses: Does not address the rural/urban divide in selective-track access documented at baseline (F-live-demography-03), since it only reforms the exam faced by those who already apply
Ignores the mechanical class-size threshold problem (F-live-demography-05): coaching-resistant admissions criteria do not change whether a locality can form a viable intake class as cohorts shrink
